dailyJudgementSnippet: Anthropic led the day because HN gave it both the broadest and the most favorable direct discussion: commenters praised the company for publishing coding-skills research, repeatedly treated Claude/Opus as the benchmark in adjacent model and agent threads, and only meaningfully dinged it on Claude token burn in OpenClaw experiments [E1][E2][E3][E4].
winnerExplanation: Anthropic won on a mix of coverage and tone. It had several separate front-page threads with meaningful direct signal: one praised Anthropic for running and publishing research on AI-assisted coding skill formation, two others used Claude Code and Opus as the standard competitors were trying to reach, and another gave Claude Code favorable marks for its permission system. The main offset was a concrete complaint that Claude token costs can spiral fast in OpenClaw, but that drag was outweighed by the larger volume of favorable and benchmark-setting mentions [E1][E2][E3][E4].

openai.judgementSnippet: Codex drew a clear quality complaint: a commenter said the generated unit tests looked fine initially but turned into a cleanup burden once inspected closely and extended [E5].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Praise centered on Anthropic publishing a coding-skills study and on Claude/Opus being treated as the bar to match in other AI threads, though OpenClaw users also warned that Claude token spend can ramp up very quickly [E1][E2][E3][E4].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: A small but direct signal came from the OpenClaw thread, where Gemini 3 Flash and Pro were singled out as the only models one user could get to use tools consistently without looping [E6].

evidence.summary: In Anthropic's coding-skills research thread, commenters praised the company for designing and publishing the study and said the data usefully backed concerns about AI assistance eroding learning.

OpenClaw commenters reported burning through Claude tokens quickly, including claims of about $5 in 30 minutes and $560 over a weekend, even while acknowledging Claude Code as a strong baseline agent.
In the Kimi K2.5 thread, commenters described Opus and Claude Code as the standard an open model was finally starting to compete with, signaling respect for Anthropic's model quality.
The Amla Sandbox discussion cited Claude Code's non-yolo mode as having a pretty good permission system, using it as a favorable comparison in a sandboxing debate.
In the 'Code is cheap' thread, a commenter said Codex-produced unit tests seemed acceptable at first but turned out to contain many bad choices once reviewed and extended.
In the OpenClaw thread, one user said Gemini 3 Flash and Pro were the only models they could consistently get to use tools without going into loops.
